title: Khobz
subtitle: Moroccan round flatbread
excerpt: The everyday bread of Morocco: a round, soft, slightly dense flatbread baked at high heat. Torn and used as a utensil for scooping tagines, dipping into salads, and eating alongside couscous. The crumb is open enough to be pillowy but sturdy enough to hold sauce. Made with a mix of plain flour and semolina.
sourceNotes: Khobz (also spelled khoubz or kesra depending on region) is the standard Moroccan leavened bread, baked twice daily in traditional homes. The round shape is characteristic across the Maghreb. The addition of semolina gives the bread its characteristic slight density and yellow tinge.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked. Refresh in a 180°C oven for 8 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Best on the day of baking.

content.text: Khobz has a soft, open crumb and a thin crust that does not harden. The high proportion of plain flour to semolina keeps it light; reversing the ratio (more semolina) produces a denser, grainier bread typical of the Berber tradition. Both are correct.

The dough should be soft and slightly sticky after mixing — do not add too much flour when kneading or the bread will be dense. An 8-minute knead by hand (or 5 minutes in a stand mixer) is sufficient. The bread is done when it sounds hollow when tapped on the base.

Combine the {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{semolina}}, {{yeast-dried}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a large bowl. Make a well in the centre. Add the {{olive-oil}} and {{water}} and mix to a shaggy dough. Turn out onto an unfloured surface and knead for 8 minutes until smooth and slightly tacky. Return to a clean oiled bowl, cover with cling film, and leave to rise for 1 hour until doubled. Divide the dough in two. Shape each half into a round roughly 20 cm across and 2 cm thick. Dust a baking tray with semolina and transfer the rounds onto it. Cover loosely and leave for 30 minutes to prove. Heat the oven to 230°C. Prick each round several times with a fork. Bake for 18 to 20 minutes until golden and hollow-sounding when tapped on the base.

items.fix: Allow the full 1-hour initial rise — the dough should double in volume. Semolina limits gluten development; if the bread is consistently dense, reduce semolina to 50 g

items.cause: Oven too hot or baked too long
items.fix: Reduce temperature to 220°C and check at 15 minutes. The crust should be pale gold, not dark brown

items.cause: Too much water or low-protein flour
items.fix: Lightly oil your hands rather than adding more flour. A soft dough produces a better crumb than a stiff one

content.text: In Morocco, khobz is baked fresh twice daily in traditional households and bought from the public bread oven (ferran) in the medina by families who carry their shaped dough from home to be baked communally. Bread is central to Moroccan eating: it is both food and utensil, used to scoop every dish on the table. No Moroccan meal begins without it. In Britain, khobz is a practical alternative to pitta for serving with Moroccan food, and the round shape makes it easy to tear and share at the table.
